back: The hypothesis that any function which is effectively computable (by an algorithm) is computable by a Turing Machine. It is a statement about the intuitive notion of computability, not a theorem that can be proven.
A decision problem for which there exists a Turing Machine that will always halt (either accept or reject) for every input string. Its language is recursive.

front: What is a Recognizable (Recursively Enumerable) Problem?
back: A decision problem for which there exists a Turing Machine that will halt and accept for every string in the language, but may either reject or loop forever on strings not in the language. Its language is recursively enumerable.

The problem of determining, from a description of an arbitrary program and its input, whether the program will finish running (halt) or continue to run forever. It was proven by Alan Turing to be undecidable.
Every non-trivial semantic property of a Turing-recognizable language is undecidable. A 'non-trivial property' is one that is neither true for every language nor false for every language.
